But damage numbers that are simple and easy to add to any enemy in your game.
We will create our own custom node that we can add like any other node through the add node window,
which will hold all the logic for spawning in the damage number label.
We do so go to the Scripts tab, then go to File, New Script, name it Damage Number Spawner,
and press Create.
Inside we will first make sure it extends node 2D.
This is because we want the position value of the node 2D,
and so when we add this custom node it will have all the properties of what it extends,
allowing us to position this spawner where we want the numbers to appear.
Next we define the class name as damage number spawner. This will turn the script into a node inside
of the add node window and the name on the right is the name of the custom node and therefore
is what we search for when we are adding this node to the scene. Next we will establish two export
variables. Label settings will hold all the settings for the label that we are spawning in
as the label settings resource holds a bunch of different data such as font, font size,
font color, outlines and more. And critical hit color will be the color of the font when
we land a critical hit which we will use a default value of red although here you
you would want to change this color to whatever color you're commonly using, which can either
be done using color, dot, and then the name of that color, or you can use a string for
the hex value, which if you do it this way then you can click on the color for an easier
color selection.
Additionally, we make both of these export variables to allow for a different value
on each spawner that we add to our game, allowing different enemies to have different
looking labels that display the damage dealt to them, which can become especially
useful if you want to use a different color for the damage dealt to the opponent,
compared to damage that an opponent does to you.
Additionally, feel free to add as many export variables as you would like with
various properties and things that we will be setting in the script. Next, we will create a custom
function called spawn label, which will have two arguments. Number is the value that we want
the label to display. And critical hit is what we will use to know whether the color should be
changed to a critical hit color, which we provide this argument with a default value of false, but
that if you have certain enemies or objects that can't receive a critical hit, then you only
need to call this function with a number and nothing else. Insert the function we will
first define a variable called new label, which will hold a new label node. Then we will
set a variety of properties on the label. For the text, we use str to convert the number
into a string that we can use for the text property. Which for this, we also use a Turner
Reef statement where we will use the exact number if step decimals with the number in brackets is
equal to zero. Also, we will use an integer version of the number step decimals simply
equals a number based on how many decimals it takes to reach the decimal that isn't zero.
So if a number is equal to 10.0, it returns zero because there is no non zero decimal.
And if a number equals 10.5, then it returns one. This saves us from having the label
display 10.0 instead displaying the number as an integer meaning it would just display 10.
We will then set the level settings of the label to a duplicate version of the label settings
export variable. This is to ensure that if we change the color to a critical hit color,
it doesn't change it on all levels using this level settings. I then set the z-index to something
like 1000 so that the label appears above all other content in the game. Although you may
want to change this to a even higher number based on how your game is set up with the
z-index. The most important part about the z-index number is just to make sure that this
label appears above everything else. We set the pivot ratio to the bottom middle of the label,
so that when we change and animate the scale of the label, the origin will be at the bottom
middle. Also, we didn't set the global position of the label node, as we will be adding a label
as a child of this spawner, which will make this label 00 equal to the exact position of
this spawner. We will however have to add a slight offset to align the pivot offset to the
00 of the spawner, which we will do later. Next, if we lend a critical hit, then we
grab the labels label settings, its font color, and change it to the critical hit color.
Also, make sure you are editing the levels, level settings, and not the level settings
export variable.
Additionally, in the case that your game has any lighting and you don't want the level
to be affected by that 2D lighting, then you can create a variable to hold a new level
material, set it to a new canvas item material, set the light mode of the material, set the
light mode unshaded, then apply that level material to the material of the level node.
This will ignore lighting and shadows from the various 2D lighting nodes, although this
part is completely optional.
Finally, we add the level node as a child of the spawner node, and we use
called a third to add the label node at the end of the current frame so that we can use
a weight on the resize signal, which will occur or emit naturally when the label changes
size due to its text being set, allowing us to then set the offset of the position of
the label node based on the size of the label so that the bottom middle of the label is
positioned at the spawn is zero zero. And by using called a third, we ensure that
the order of events are correct and don't cause any potential errors. Additionally,
if you would like a slight offset to the spawn position of the label, you can also
add this line, which we'll just add a random value from negative five to five on both axis.
Now for the animation of the label node, we will establish three variables to help.
Target rise position is the position we want the label to reach so that it looks like it's rising.
This will equal the label's current position plus a random value from negative 22 to negative 16
on the Y so that it will go up. By having a different value, it will appear as if the
animation is rising at different speeds and we also apply a slight randomness of the
horizontal position of the target so that the value doesn't just go straight up, allowing
the player to differentiate between multiple damaged labels that may appear at very close times.
Wean length is the total amount of time that the animation will last, and because the animation
will last for a set amount of time, it will therefore create an illusion of certain labels
having to rise faster than others, due to them having to reach a higher position,
which is again why we provide some randomness to the vertical target position.
Then to actually animate this label, we create a variable called label tween,
which will hold a newly created tween, which a tween simply grabs a property on a node
and moves it towards a value over a given amount of time and by default a tween uses a linear
progression towards this target value so to provide a better or more smoother looking animation
I will use set transition to set the tween to a transition of back now I'll set the easing
the ease out feel free to change these values as you would like to change how the animation
looks now to activate the tween and provide the necessary data we will be using the
tween property function three times grabbing the level node the property that we want to
affect the target value and finally the total time of the tween animation which we use
use our twin length variable for.

Now, when I want to add a new label settings, which has all the typical values that I use,

And if you just want to use the majority of settings in this typical label settings,
but you want to change maybe a few things for a specific enemy, then select the drop down again
and press make unique so that now any edits made won't affect the label settings file that
we just saved. Once all the values are set as you would like for the visuals of the label
being spawned, we can go to the script of the enemy and all we need to do is grab the
the damage number spawn a node that we just created, call the spawn label function, passing
the amount of damage that we want to deal, which you can just put a number here if you
would like, and whether it is a critical hit, which you can just put a true or false here
if you would like as well. Keep in mind that if you have a certain enemy or object that
doesn't receive a critical hit damage, then you can just pass the damage on its own,
as Godot will just rely on the default value that we set earlier for the critical
hit variable, which was false, and again just to clear any confusion, you don't
have to use separate variables for the damage or critical hit, as you can just pass
a number with or without a decimal and a true or false for the critical hit.
Now you have a simple damage number spawner that you can add to any of your enemies in
any of your 2D CoD games and don't forget that you can check out the project files
in the description.
